name: Deposition Impeachment Builder
summary: This skill drafts deposition-ready impeachment sequences using the Commit–Credit–Confront method, with special emphasis on building a reliable foundation for the prior statement and preserving a clean record for later use. It guides the executing LLM to diagnose whether a contradiction is material, draft controlled commitment and confrontation questions, and tailor credit foundations to the specific statement type (sworn testimony, verified responses, emails, corporate records, etc.). The skill embeds evidentiary, procedural, and ethics guardrails—including citation accuracy requirements, authentication protocols, privilege awareness, and anti-hallucination constraints—and requires attorney review before any use in proceedings.
content:
# Deposition Impeachment Builder: The Commit–Credit–Confront Framework

## 1. Opening Context

This skill produces a deposition-ready impeachment sequence using the classic Commit–Credit–Confront method: first locking the witness into their current testimony, then establishing the reliability and adoption of a prior statement, and finally confronting them with the inconsistency in a controlled, evidentiary-safe manner. Done well, impeachment is one of the highest-leverage deposition tools: it narrows the witness's room to maneuver, preserves clean clips for summary judgment or trial, and can materially change settlement value. Done poorly, it educates the witness, invites rehabilitative explanations, creates avoidable record ambiguity, or—worst of all—fails to authenticate the prior statement so it becomes unusable later.

The professional standards here are practical rather than ornamental. The sequence must be fair, accurate, and supported by an identifiable source. It must comply with governing deposition rules, evidentiary rules on impeachment, and ethical rules governing truthfulness and dealings with represented persons. The output of this skill is not a script to read blindly. It is attorney work product that must be adapted to the witness, the forum, and the specific objective of the examination, and it requires attorney review before any use in a proceeding.

Scope boundary: this skill focuses on designing and drafting the Commit–Credit–Confront questioning sequence and related tactical guidance for depositions and similar sworn examinations. It does not replace broader deposition planning (topic outlines, exhibit binders, privilege strategy, time allocation) and does not provide jurisdiction-specific legal advice. Any use in court filings or at trial requires separate admissibility analysis under the applicable evidence rules and governing pretrial orders.

## 2. Understanding the Context and Purpose

Impeachment in deposition serves at least three distinct purposes, and your strategy should be clear about which one you are pursuing.

First, it preserves a record for later use. A clean impeachment sequence can support summary judgment by showing no genuine dispute on a material fact, narrow issues for trial, or undermine credibility before the factfinder. In this mode, your goal is a transcript that reads unambiguously: short questions, single factual propositions, and a tight chain to the prior statement.

Second, it can drive discovery and admissions. A controlled confrontation often flushes out additional details and identifies documents, conversations, or other witnesses. Here, your goal is not merely contradiction; it is a roadmap: who, what, when, where, and the documents that would corroborate either version.

Third, it can reposition the negotiation posture. Selective, well-timed impeachment signals to opposing counsel that a narrative is unstable, encouraging stipulations or settlement movement. In this mode, you must be especially conscious of not overplaying your hand: you want to demonstrate strength without giving the witness a full rehearsal for trial.

Your audience is broader than the witness. The transcript will be read by opposing counsel preparing the witness for trial and possible rehabilitation, by the court on motion practice, and potentially by a jury via reading or video clips if the deposition is used at trial. Those readers expect clarity, fairness, and rule-compliant foundations. They punish sloppiness—especially misquoting, ambiguous references, or argumentative questions that invite objections and nonresponsive answers.

Impeachment also serves two analytically distinct evidentiary purposes: it destroys the witness's credibility (general impeachment), and in certain circumstances it can supply substantive evidence of the truth of the prior statement. Under Federal Rule of Evidence 801(d)(1)(A), a prior inconsistent statement given under penalty of perjury at a trial, hearing, or other proceeding or in a deposition is not hearsay and may be used substantively. A party-opponent's own prior statement may also be admissible substantively under FRE 801(d)(2). Whether the prior statement is usable only for impeachment or also substantively is a critical strategic determination that affects how you build the Credit phase and what you do with the confrontation afterward.

## 3. Prerequisites and Information Gathering

Before drafting any impeachment sequence, the executing LLM must have enough information to accurately quote both statements, evaluate whether the inconsistency is real and material, and lay a proper foundation so the confrontation is usable later. The LLM must ask the user for any missing elements before proceeding to the drafting phase and must never fabricate quotes, dates, page numbers, or document attributes.

At minimum, the following inputs are required.

The "Current Testimony" must be obtained as it will appear in the deposition record. The preferred input is the exact transcript excerpt (question and answer with line numbers if available) or a verbatim anticipated answer. If the testimony is only a paraphrase, the LLM must ask the user to confirm the precise phrasing they intend to elicit during the Commit phase, because impeachment depends on exactness.

The "Prior Statement" must be obtained with source details sufficient to authenticate and use it later. The LLM should request: the full text of the prior statement (not just a snippet); the type of statement (prior deposition testimony, interrogatory response, signed declaration, email, text message, internal report, pleading, social media post, etc.); the date; the author or speaker; the recipients or audience; how it was created and stored; and whether it was reviewed, approved, signed, adopted, sent, or otherwise ratified by the witness. Pin-cite information is essential: for deposition testimony, this means volume, page, and line numbers; for documents, paragraph or section identifiers.

Case context is also necessary to judge materiality and timing. The LLM should ask for: the jurisdiction and forum (federal court vs. specific state court); case posture (fact discovery, expert discovery, summary judgment, pretrial); the claims, defenses, and the specific element or issue the impeachment relates to; whether the witness is a party, corporate designee (e.g., a Rule 30(b)(6) designee in federal practice), percipient witness, or expert; and whether the attorney intends to use the prior statement as impeachment only or also substantively.

Finally, logistical and record considerations should be gathered: whether this is a video deposition; whether exhibits will be marked electronically; whether a protective order or confidentiality designation applies to the prior statement; and any known ground rules from the parties' deposition protocol.

If any required information is missing—especially the full prior statement text or the witness's connection to it—the LLM must pause and ask targeted follow-up questions before drafting.

## 4. Step-by-Step Process

### Step 1: Diagnose the Inconsistency and Assess Whether It Is Worth the Capital

Not every discrepancy is impeachable in a useful way. Start by classifying the contradiction. The most deposition-effective contradictions are binary and material: "I did not attend the meeting" versus "I attended the meeting." More fragile contradictions include differences in degree ("rarely" vs. "sometimes"), scope ("I didn't see it" vs. "I saw part of it"), or recollection ("I don't recall" vs. a prior statement reflecting actual memory). The LLM should explicitly identify whether the discrepancy is a direct factual contradiction, an omission (prior silence where you would expect mention), an inconsistency in detail (numbers, dates, participants), an inconsistency in claimed knowledge (then knew vs. now doesn't know), or an inconsistency between testimony and a document authored or received by the witness.

This classification matters because each category suggests a different confrontation technique. A direct contradiction supports a tight confrontation. An omission requires care because the witness can argue the prior statement was not intended to be exhaustive. A "lack of recollection" inconsistency can be powerful but requires showing a foundation for why the witness should have remembered earlier and why the earlier statement reflects actual memory rather than guesswork.

You should also test materiality. Tie the contradiction to a claim element, defense element, damages component, or credibility theme. If you cannot articulate in one sentence why the contradiction matters to a judge or jury, consider banking it for cross-examination at trial rather than spending deposition time. A mere nuance or clarification is not impeachment; the contradiction must be material.

Adversarial awareness: opposing counsel will try to reframe the difference as semantics, context, or misunderstanding. The LLM should anticipate the most likely escape routes—"I was confused by the question," "I was only talking about a specific part of the event," "my memory has since been refreshed"—and design the Commit phase to close them by defining terms, narrowing the timeframe, and identifying the specific actors and events.

### Step 2: Build the Commit Phase—Lock the Witness Into a Clean, Quotable Proposition

The goal of the Commit phase is to eliminate every possible escape hatch before the witness knows a contradiction is coming. You must make the witness double down on their current testimony so they cannot later claim confusion, misunderstanding, or that they were referring to something else.

Quality execution requires tight, leading questions that repeat the witness's testimony back to them with absolute clarity. Use the witness's own vocabulary where possible. Avoid compound questions. Each question should contain a single factual proposition.

First, anchor the who, what, when, and where. If the contradiction involves a meeting, specify the date, location, attendees, and subject. If it involves a document, identify it by title, date, and author. Then ask the core proposition.

Second, define ambiguous terms before committing. If the witness says "I never approved," define "approve" (sign? verbal authorization? instruct someone to send?). Do this neutrally, then commit.

Third, obtain an unqualified answer and confirm certainty. If the witness hedges, clarify the hedge into a second committed proposition. These "forking" questions create impeachment strength. Confirm that the witness is certain, that their memory is clear, and that there is no other information that would change their answer.

Illustrative Commit sequence (example language to be customized to the facts):

"Just so I understand your testimony: you did not attend the March 3, 2024 project kickoff meeting, correct?"

"You're not saying you attended part of it—you're saying you did not attend at all, right?"

"You're certain about that? There's no doubt in your mind?"

"And you did not speak with anyone who attended that meeting about what happened there, correct?"

Common mistakes include committing to a paraphrase rather than the witness's own words, letting the witness give a narrative that bakes in their explanation, asking argumentative questions ("So you're saying you lied earlier?") that invite objections and rehabilitation, and moving too quickly without fully closing the escape routes.

### Step 3: Draft the Credit Phase—Establish Reliability, Authorship, Adoption, and Context

The Credit phase is the most neglected step in junior practice and is where this skill provides the most value. Before the witness knows which statement you are referring to, you must force them to agree that the version of events they are about to be confronted with is the more reliable one. You are building up the prior statement's trustworthiness and the witness's ownership of it so that they cannot later escape with "I didn't write it," "I didn't read it," "it's out of context," or "someone else drafted it."

Because "prior statement" can mean many things, the LLM must identify the category and generate a tailored Credit foundation.

If the prior statement is prior sworn testimony (deposition, hearing, trial), credit focuses on the oath, opportunity to be accurate, and lack of coercion. Establish that the witness was under oath, understood the obligation to tell the truth, had their lawyer present, had the chance to ask for clarification if any question was confusing, and answered based on their knowledge at the time. If there was an errata sheet, establish whether changes were made to the specific testimony at issue. Sample language: "You remember giving a deposition in this case on [date]?" "You were under oath then, just as you are today?" "You swore to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth?" "Your lawyer was sitting right next to you?" "You had the chance to review and correct that transcript afterward?" "And you didn't make any changes to the testimony we are about to discuss?"

If the witness tries to devalue the prior statement during the Credit phase ("I was tired that day"), address it immediately: "You wouldn't have given false testimony just because you were tired, right?" "If you were too tired to be accurate, you would have told us and we would have stopped the deposition, correct?"

If the prior statement is a signed declaration, affidavit, or verified response, credit focuses on signature, review, understanding, and intent to make a truthful statement under penalty of perjury where applicable.

If the prior statement is an email, text, or document authored by the witness, credit focuses on authorship, account ownership, routine practice, contemporaneity, recipients, and whether the content reflects what the witness knew at the time. If drafted by someone else but approved by the witness, credit focuses on review and adoption. Sample language: "The email address john.smith@company.com is your work email, correct?" "You used that account in March 2024 in the ordinary course of your job?" "You recognize this email chain dated March 4, 2024?" "That's your signature block at the bottom?" "You sent this email to Maria Lopez and copied Alex Chen?" "You sent it the day after the project kickoff meeting?" "And at the time you sent it, you intended the recipients to rely on it as an accurate description of the status?"

If the prior statement is an interrogatory response or other discovery response, credit focuses on the witness's role in preparing it, whether it was verified, and what steps were taken to ensure accuracy. This is especially important when responses are drafted by counsel: you must establish whether the witness reviewed and adopted them.

If the prior statement is a corporate record or third-party document, credit may require a different approach. You may not be able to attribute it to the witness as their statement, but you can use it to test their credibility ("you were copied," "you received it," "you relied on it") or to refresh recollection. The LLM should flag this as a different evidentiary posture and not overpromise "impeachment" when the real dynamic is "contradiction by document."

Common mistakes include skipping authentication and jumping to the confrontation, asking one broad credit question ("you wrote this, right?") without locking down key attributes (date, recipients, whether the witness reviewed the document), and allowing the witness to "poison" the statement before you confront ("I didn't mean it," "that was sarcasm"). You generally want to delay interpretive commentary until after the statement is read into the record.

### Step 4: Execute the Confront Phase—Lock, Show, Read, and Compare

The Confront phase is the climax. It must be short, punchy, and devoid of any "why" questions. You are not asking the witness to explain the contradiction; you are presenting it as an undeniable fact. The confrontation should follow a disciplined rhythm.

First, re-commit. Immediately before confronting, briefly re-anchor the current testimony so the record shows the "before" and "after" in close proximity.

Second, mark and orient. If using an exhibit, mark it (or identify it by Bates number) and confirm the witness is looking at the same page and line. In remote depositions, include transcript-friendly directions: "Let me know when you're on page 2," "Do you see the sentence beginning…"

Third, read the exact words into the record. Do not summarize. The confrontation should quote verbatim and include the location (page and line or paragraph). If the user has not provided page and line numbers, the LLM must include a placeholder (e.g., "[PAGE:LINE]") and instruct the attorney to complete it before use.

Fourth, obtain the contradiction admission. Ask whether the witness made that statement (or sent that email) and whether you read it correctly. The LLM should offer two variants: a conservative version focused on establishing the inconsistency ("So your testimony today is different from what you said under oath on [date], correct?") and an aggressive version seeking a "then or now" truth commitment ("Which is accurate—your email from March 4, 2024, or your testimony today?").

Illustrative Confront language for prior deposition testimony:

"Earlier you testified that you did not attend the March 3, 2024 kickoff meeting at all. Correct?"

"I'm handing you what has been marked as Exhibit [__], your prior deposition transcript. Please turn to page [__], line [__]. Tell me when you're there."

"I'll read the question and your answer: Question: 'Did you attend the March 3 kickoff meeting?' Answer: 'Yes, I was there for the first half hour.' Did I read that correctly?"

"That was your testimony under oath at the time, correct?"

"So as we sit here today, your testimony is different from what you said under oath on [date], correct?"

Illustrative Confront language for an email:

"Earlier you testified you did not attend the kickoff meeting. Correct?"

"I'm showing you Exhibit [__], an email dated March 4, 2024 from your account to Maria Lopez, copied to Alex Chen. Do you see that?"

"I'm going to read one sentence: 'Yesterday's kickoff meeting was productive; I will circulate the action items we agreed on.' Did I read that accurately?"

"You sent that email, correct?"

"So as of March 4, 2024, you represented to your colleagues that you attended the kickoff meeting, correct?"

After the confrontation, stop. Do not ask "Why did you change your story?" or "Were you lying then or are you lying now?" Those open-ended questions allow the witness to give a long-winded explanation that dilutes the impact. The silence after the confrontation is where the damage is done.

Common mistakes include confronting without re-committing to the current testimony immediately beforehand, letting the witness argue about "context" before the contradiction is established, and failing to read the precise language into the record.

Adversarial awareness: witnesses often respond with "I don't recall," "that's not what I meant," or "someone else wrote that." The LLM should include follow-on containment questions: "What part is inaccurate?" "What did you mean by 'we agreed'?" "Who wrote it?" "When did you first realize it was wrong?" These follow-ups create impeachment depth and can support later claims of recent fabrication.

### Step 5: Choose the Right Deployment Moment—Impeach Now Versus Bank It

Timing is a strategic choice that depends on your objectives and the type of witness.

If your objective is discovery, early impeachment can be useful because it forces the witness to explain and identify sources, documents, and conversations. Confronting early may open a line of questioning about the witness's role and produce additional custodians or documents.

If your objective is trial-quality impeachment, consider banking. Sometimes you commit the witness, move on, and confront later—after they have made multiple aligned denials—so the contradiction is starker. Banking is also useful when you want to avoid educating the witness on the existence or significance of a document that will be more valuable at trial. This is the classic "pocket impeachment."

If the witness is likely to be prepared with a repair narrative (for example, a sophisticated executive with counsel coaching), consider partial credit without full confrontation: establish authorship and routine practice but do not read the damaging line yet, saving the moment for trial.

Witness type also matters. For a party or aligned employee, impeachment can reshape the case theme. For a neutral third-party, overly aggressive impeachment can backfire; often a gentle confrontation that preserves cooperation is more effective. If the witness is a "pathological" liar, saving several impeachments for a rapid-fire sequence can be devastating. If the witness is generally credible but has made one major error, impeach immediately to signal strength and make the witness more compliant for the remainder of the deposition.

Common mistakes include impeaching on trivia, impeaching too early and giving the witness time to adapt, and failing to connect the contradiction to a later motion or theme.

### Step 6: Convert the Impeachment Into Usable Record

After confrontation, do not simply move on. Use the moment to obtain one of three things: an admission, a narrowed dispute, or a roadmap for further discovery.

If the witness concedes the prior statement was accurate, lock it in with a clean statement: "So you did attend the meeting for the first half hour." If the witness concedes today's testimony was mistaken, capture it without editorializing.

If the witness refuses to choose, narrow the dispute: "Your position is you attended some portion but not the full meeting?" "You're not disputing you sent the email; you're disputing what you meant by 'we agreed'?" These concessions can be as valuable as a full reversal.

If the witness claims misunderstanding or new information, extract the sources: "When did you learn that?" "Who told you?" "What document did you review?" "Where is that reflected?" This creates follow-up document requests and third-party subpoenas.

The LLM should also include guidance for handling privilege landmines. If the witness suggests their changed testimony is based on discussions with counsel, the examiner should pivot to nonprivileged facts: timing, documents reviewed, and communications with non-lawyers. It is improper to solicit privileged communications, and privilege assertions should be respected while still developing the factual basis for the changed testimony.

## 5. Critical Legal Standards and Compliance

This skill implicates evidentiary rules, civil procedure, and professional responsibility.

Regarding the evidentiary framework, impeachment by prior inconsistent statement is governed in federal practice principally by Federal Rule of Evidence 613. FRE 613(a) provides that when examining a witness about a prior statement, the examiner need not show it or disclose its contents to the witness, but must show or disclose it to an adverse party's attorney on request. FRE 613(b) provides that extrinsic evidence of a prior inconsistent statement is admissible only if the witness is given an opportunity to explain or deny the statement and the adverse party is given an opportunity to examine the witness about it—or if justice so requires. Related hearsay rules affect whether the statement can be used substantively: FRE 801(d)(1)(A) exempts from hearsay a declarant-witness's prior inconsistent statement that was given under penalty of perjury at a trial, hearing, other proceeding, or in a deposition, and FRE 801(d)(2) exempts opposing party statements. The skill must not assume admissibility; it should frame confrontation to preserve all options and instruct that the distinction between substantive use and impeachment-only use requires attorney analysis.

The Rule of Completeness under FRE 106 is also relevant. If you introduce only a portion of a prior statement, the opposing party may require introduction of other parts that in fairness ought to be considered at the same time. The LLM should remind the attorney to review the full context of the prior statement and be prepared for a completeness objection.

Regarding deposition procedure, in federal civil cases deposition conduct and objections are governed principally by FRCP 30(c)(2), which limits objections during depositions to those on the grounds of relevance and privilege, and FRCP 30(d)(3), which permits a party or the deponent to move to terminate or limit a deposition on the ground that it is being conducted in bad faith or to unreasonably annoy, embarrass, or oppress the deponent. Instructions not to answer are limited to narrow circumstances: privilege, a court-ordered limitation, or a motion under Rule 30(d)(3). Because states vary widely in their deposition objection practices, the LLM must ask the user for the forum and flag state-specific deposition practices for attorney verification rather than guessing.

Regarding professional responsibility, the LLM should instruct compliance with the ABA Model Rules of Professional Conduct, including Rule 1.1 (competence), Rule 3.3 (candor toward the tribunal, relevant when deposition excerpts are later used in filings), Rule 3.4 (fairness to opposing party and counsel—never attempt to impeach with a document that does not actually contain a contradiction or allude to matters not supported by admissible evidence), Rule 4.1 (truthfulness in statements to others), and Rule 4.2 (communications with represented persons—this skill assumes questioning occurs in a formal proceeding with counsel present). If the witness is represented, all communications should occur through counsel.

Anti-hallucination guardrails are non-negotiable. The LLM must never invent page or line citations, quotation marks around fabricated text, dates, or document attributes. If the user has not provided a verbatim quote, the LLM must label any proposed quotation as a placeholder and require attorney verification. If the LLM references case law or legal doctrines, it must either provide a web-verified citation or label it for attorney research. All placeholders must be clearly marked (e.g., "[DATE]," "[EXHIBIT NO.]," "[PAGE:LINE]").

Malpractice risk areas include misquoting the prior statement, failing to authenticate or establish adoption, impeaching on immaterial points that damage the examiner's credibility, inadvertently waiving privilege or eliciting privileged communications, and failing to have the prior statement properly authenticated or enough copies for the witness, the court, and opposing counsel. Mitigation includes disciplined exhibit handling, verbatim reading, clear record markers, and a privilege-aware follow-up plan.

Confidentiality and protective orders: if the prior statement is produced under a protective order, the attorney must follow designation and handling requirements and avoid embedding confidential information in publicly filed exhibits without following applicable sealing procedures.

## 6. Quality Control and Review

Before using the generated sequence, the attorney must perform a substantive review evaluating accuracy, logic, foundation, control, and strategic fit.

Start with accuracy. Confirm that both the Current Testimony and the Prior Statement are quoted verbatim from the record or document and that the sequence does not paraphrase key language. If page, line, or paragraph citations are available, ensure they are correct. If not, the output should explicitly flag missing pinpoint citations as needing completion before use. Hallucinating a page number during a deposition destroys the attorney's credibility instantly.

Then test the logic. Does the Commit proposition match what the prior statement actually contradicts? Many failed impeachments arise from mismatched propositions—committing to "never" when the prior statement only shows "once," or committing to "did not know" when the prior statement shows "heard." Rewrite the Commit question until it aligns tightly.

Next, test foundation. Does the Credit phase establish enough that a judge would later view the prior statement as fairly attributable to the witness and reliable? For emails, is authorship and adoption locked down? For counsel-drafted discovery responses, did the witness review and verify? For prior testimony, are the oath and identity established?

Then test control. Do the Confront questions preserve examiner control (short, single fact, transcript-friendly) and avoid inviting speeches? Are there planned containment follow-ups if the witness evades? Ensure no questions in the sequence ask "why," "how," or "could it be." The sequence must be composed entirely of closed-ended, leading questions.

Finally, test strategic fit. Does this impeachment advance a case theme or element? Does it occur at the right time in the deposition outline? Would it be better banked for trial? The output should flag strategic tradeoffs rather than pretend there is a single correct choice.

All outputs from this skill require attorney review before use in any deposition, motion, or trial. The skill does not constitute legal advice, does not create an attorney-client relationship, and cannot substitute for jurisdiction-specific professional judgment.

## 7. Jurisdiction-Specific Adaptations

While the Commit–Credit–Confront method is nearly universal, procedural requirements vary by jurisdiction, and the LLM should ask for the forum before finalizing any sequence.

In federal court, the skill should default to the FRCP 30 framework for deposition conduct and to the Federal Rules of Evidence for impeachment, especially FRE 613 and FRE 801. Be mindful of the collateral matter doctrine: courts generally will not permit extrinsic evidence to impeach a witness on a minor, irrelevant point. The impeachment must go to a material issue or the witness's general credibility. Admissibility at trial may depend on additional rules (authentication, completeness, hearsay exceptions), and the LLM should flag these for attorney analysis.

In California, Evidence Code Section 770 explicitly allows the judge to exclude extrinsic evidence of an inconsistent statement unless the witness was given an opportunity to explain or deny the statement while testifying. California practitioners should be prepared for the judge to require or allow an immediate explanation, and the Credit and Confront phases should account for this possibility. Evidence Code Section 780 sets forth the factors relevant to witness credibility, including prior inconsistent statements.

In New York, CPLR 4514 allows any party to introduce a prior inconsistent statement made in writing or under oath for impeachment purposes, but imposes stricter limits on the use of prior inconsistent statements that are not in writing or not under oath.

In jurisdictions following the common-law doctrine from Browne v. Dunn, the examining party must "put their case" to the witness—explicitly confronting them with the contradiction and giving them a chance to respond before relying on the prior statement in closing argument. This doctrine reinforces the importance of a complete Confront phase.

If the matter involves arbitration, provider rules (AAA, JAMS, etc.) and the arbitrator's scheduling order may govern deposition use and evidentiary standards. The LLM should treat arbitration as a separate regime and request the governing rules before optimizing the confrontation for later use.

If the witness is a corporate designee (e.g., under FRCP 30(b)(6)), preparation obligations and the binding effect of testimony may differ across jurisdictions. The LLM should avoid asserting binding effects without verification and should flag any such statements for attorney review.

Because local court rules, judge-specific practices, and state-specific deposition conduct standards are highly variable, the LLM should flag any aggressive tone choices or assumptions about objection practices for attorney adjustment to local norms.

---

## Output Specification

When executed with the required inputs, the LLM should generate three deliverables: first, a tailored Commit–Credit–Confront script with optional conservative and aggressive variants; second, a short deployment memo explaining when to use the impeachment, what it accomplishes, and how opposing counsel is likely to respond; and third, a list of missing information that must be filled (pin cites, exhibit identifiers, adoption facts) before use. All placeholders must be clearly labeled and the LLM must not fabricate any factual details.
